data.title: Aiding the abduction of Ukrainian children must come with consequences
data.description:
“They do not like the light of day. They’re like vampires,” former U.S. War Crimes Ambassador David Crane said about private corporations and their leaders that facilitate war crimes.  

At a hearing of the Tom Lantos Human Rights Commission in the U.S. Capitol last month, witnesses and members of Congress considered that Russian President Vladimir Putin is unlikely to face the consequences from international legal bodies for crimes against humanity, including Russia’s abduction of Ukrainian children. 

But, as Crane and other witnesses pointed out, it’s time for lower-level government officials and executives at Russian companies that aid and abet child abductions to face consequences from the United States and other opposing countries.  

The hearing came on the same day that Yale School of Public Health’s Humanitarian Research Lab released its latest study, finding that Russian energy giants Rosneft and Gazprom contributed to the deportation, indoctrination and militarization of Ukrainian children.  

According to the report, the two companies, along with their subsidiaries and trade unions, “underwrote and facilitated the transport” of approximately 2,158 children from Russian-occupied Ukrainian territory to at least six summer camps inside Russia and Russian-occupied Ukraine, three of which were owned by Gazprom subsidiaries.

Russia’s oil and gas sector is projected to account for over 22 percent of Russia’s federal budget in 2026 and is essential to sustaining Russia’s ongoing invasion. Rosneft and Gazprom are the sector’s largest companies, led by some of Putin’s closest allies.  

The allegations implicate the two companies in Russia’s well-documented crime of abducting Ukrainian children. International law considers the transfer of children from an occupied territory to the territory of the occupying power to be a war crime, regardless of motive.  

Ukraine estimates that nearly 20,000 Ukrainian children have been abducted. Throughout the Russian-occupied territories, Ukrainian schoolchildren are subjected to pro-Russian “patriotic education initiatives” — part of Putin’s goal of erasing Ukrainian identity through “Russification.” 

In 2023, the International Criminal Court issued arrest warrants for Putin and Russia’s Commissioner for Children’s Rights Maria Lvova-Belova, holding them “allegedly responsible for the war crime of unlawful deportation of population (children)” and the “unlawful transfer of population (children) from occupied areas of Ukraine to the Russian Federation.”   

But although Article 27 of the Rome Statute establishing the court does not exempt heads of state from prosecution, Russia is not a signatory to the statute. Moscow obviously has no intention of handing over Putin, Lvova-Belova or any other Russian official. 

Another approach is needed to pressure those individuals and entities involved in trafficking Ukrainian children into Russia.

Out of 44 entities accused in Yale’s report that facilitated the transfer of children, a shocking 80 percent have not been sanctioned by the United States or Europe. The report listed camps, oil and gas subsidiaries, trade unions and high-level company directors.  

“Corporations are a lurking shadow behind atrocities,” Crane said, drawing on his experience prosecuting war crimes in West Africa, adding, “there’s nothing wrong with naming and shaming. It’s bad press for any corporation to be named in something like this.”  

In addition to naming and shaming, the U.S. and European governments should initiate a campaign to target senior executives one or two levels below the billionaire oligarchs, Russians who have operational control of subsidiary businesses or organizations involved in Russia’s crimes against Ukrainian children.

This cohort may not have yachts like the oligarchs, but they retain access to the West. In 2025, 49 percent of Russian foreign real estate transactions were in Europe. Russians are obtaining residence permits, the “ability to travel within the Schengen Area, open accounts in the [European Union], and manage financial resources,” according to data from the international real estate consulting firm, Intermark Global.

The U.S. saw a 78.5 percent year-on-year surge in Russian demand for real estate in the first half of 2025. Travel to the West, especially Western Europe, has also rebounded since the early days of the war.

The U.S. Treasury and State departments must target Russia’s executive class, including those listed in Yale’s report, with targeted sanctions, asset freezes and travel bans for facilitating child abductions to disincentivize their participation in the scheme.

Congress and the administration should also look to identify persons and entities engaged in this human trafficking effort and sanction them under the Global Magnitsky Act, which allows for asset freezes and visa bans for those found to be involved in human rights abuses and corruption.

Creating consequences for their participation in Russia’s atrocities may do more to curtail their actions than targeting Russia’s leadership and flagship corporations.

Dmitriy Shapiro is a research analyst and editor at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies.

data.title: Are Democrats winning elections but losing the narrative? Join the live discussion
data.description:
Democrats are winning races — and even redistricting battles — but some in the party worry they’re not yet winning the argument.

Join The Hill’s Amie Parnes and Editor in Chief Ian Swanson for a discussion Tuesday afternoon on Democrats’ messaging and political fallout from the shooting Saturday night at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

The analysis will include a Q&A session on the latest news in the early race for the White House.

The event is scheduled to begin at noon EDT.

Register and watch the live discussion above.

If you register before the event is live, you will receive an email containing the link to join once it starts. If you register as the event is starting, you will be transferred directly to the livestream.

data.title: McCabe: 'Fascinating' WHCA dinner shooting suspect 'made no effort whatsoever' to cover tracks
data.description:
Former acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe on Tuesday said it was “fascinating” to him that the suspect accused of targeting Trump administration officials at the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner over the weekend appeared to make “no effort whatsoever to cover his tracks.”  

Cole Allen, 31, was charged with attempting to assassinate President Trump and other firearm-related offenses after allegedly opening fire at the Washington Hilton hotel on Saturday.  

“He left an extensive record, certainly in the social media posts that you referred to earlier, all of which will be helpful to the prosecution in their effort to prove his intent to try to assassinate the president,” McCabe said during an interview on CNN.

“He wrote an extensive letter or manifesto or whatever you want to call it, and sent it to many people, some of whom handed it directly to law enforcement, in which he basically confessed to the crime ahead of time, explained why he was doing it, who he was targeting in what order,” he continued.

Allen was allegedly targeting White House officials “prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest,” according to a manifesto confirmed to be written by the suspect.

A White House official also told The Hill that Allen’s sister informed authorities that her brother often made “radical statements” and referenced plans to do “something” in response to the issues facing today’s world.   

Allen’s brother alerted police in New London, Conn., to the alleged manifesto just minutes before the incident occurred at the WHCA dinner on Saturday, according to the official.

Authorities said Allen was armed with a shotgun, handgun and multiple knives when he rushed the hotel where Trump, the first lady and several Cabinet officials were gathered for the annual black-tie affair with journalists.

A Secret Service agent was struck but saved by a bulletproof vest, Trump told reporters after the incident. No one inside was hurt, but the chaos sent attendees ducking for cover under tables as shots rang out.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on Sunday that the suspect traveled from Los Angeles to Washington, D.C. with plans to interrupt the dinner.

McCabe argued on Tuesday that the evidence in the case was “already quite strong,” but authorities would continue working to gather information to help better understand Allen’s motive.

“What investigators are looking for now is additional intelligence that can tell us a little bit more about why this young man went in the direction that he did,” he said. “And that‘s an important question for our intelligence and law enforcement folks to understand.”

Allen, a professional game designer with a master’s degree in computer science, made his initial appearance in federal court on Monday. He did not enter a plea.

data.title: Talarico leads Cornyn, Paxton in Texas Senate race, new poll shows
data.description:
James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for U.S. Senate in Texas, is leading both of his potential Republican opponents in hypothetical head-to-head match-ups ahead of the general election, according to a new poll.

In the Texas Public Opinion Research survey, conducted in mid-April, likely general election voters in the Lone Star State say they would back Talarico over incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R), 44 percent to 41 percent — just outside the poll’s margin of error. Another 11 percent of respondents say they’re undecided.

In a hypothetical match-up against Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R), the Democratic candidate’s advantage widens to 5 points, leading 46 percent to 41 percent, with just 9 percent undecided.

None of the Republican candidates for the Senate secured a majority of votes during the primary election, sending Cornyn and Paxton to a runoff on May 26. The winner will face Talarico in November.

In both hypothetical matchups, Talarico leads among independent voters by more than 20 percentage points. Against Cornyn, Talarico leads, 51 percent to 29 percent; and against Paxton, his advantage widens to 53 percent to 28 percent.

Similar trends are seen among moderate voters, who back Talarico by more than 40 percentage points in both hypothetical races: Talarico leads Cornyn, 62 percent to 19 percent; he leads Paxton, 65 percent to 16 percent.

Talario appears to have far outraised both potential Republican candidates since the primary last month, according to a New York Times analysis, but Cornyn holds a significant advantage over his GOP opponent.

Since the primary, Cornyn has brought in more than $6.5 million, across his campaign’s funds and an independent political action committee backing him. Paxton’s campaign and independent PAC, meanwhile, raised more than $1.9 million, the Times reported.

The survey, conducted on April 17-20, included 1,865 likely general election voters. The margin of error is 2.5 percentage points.

data.title: Watch live: Trump, first lady welcome King Charles III, Queen Camilla
data.description:
President Trump and first lady Melania Trump welcome King Charles III and Queen Camilla to the White House on Tuesday morning.

Watch the welcome ceremony live here.

data.title: No man is an island — Trump is learning that lesson the hard way
data.description:
President Trump should take advantage of last week’s traumatic domestic events to rebuild political and allied support for U.S. foreign policy initiatives. This includes a much-needed correction to his flawed overtures to Vladimir Putin on Ukraine and Xi Jinping on Taiwan.  

While pursuing their own national ambitions, Russia, China, Iran and North Korea have found it useful to form an authoritarian axis that has a common strategic purpose: to undermine and destroy the U.S.-led international order. In jointly supporting each other’s regional goals, they follow the West’s collective security principle of “all for one, one for all,” but they apply the mandate to their anti-Western geopolitical cause in accordance with the old Marxist-Leninist doctrine, “from each according to his ability, to each according to his need.”

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine was the first real test of the new anti-West agenda. China has provided weapons, drones and intelligence, while Iran delivered its domestically produced drones to support Russia. North Korean forces entered Russia to help expel Ukraine’s counter-incursion in Kursk. All three have offered diplomatic and political support for Moscow at the United Nations and in other international fora.  

The U.S.-Israel attack on Iran was the next geopolitical challenge for the authoritarian axis.  China and Russia are sending Iran weapons and air defense systems to help fend off the U.S. assault. Moscow has provided Teheran with targeting information to kill Americans in the Gulf states, and is presently hosting an Iranian delegation to coordinate strategy. It remains to be seen what North Korea’s contribution to the anti-U.S. effort in Iran may be; they could send troops, battle-hardened after their experience in Russia, to be stationed at coastal positions to resist a U.S. ground force landing.

The anti-U.S. coalition has also helped the authoritarian cause in less direct ways, by requiring the U.S. to divide its attention and resources among multiple target areas. The U.S. is expending its store of externally available Tomahawk missiles exclusively against Iran, when some could well be deployed for Ukraine’s use to strike strategic targets deep within Russia. Other weapons systems reserved for the Iran effort may be needed if (or when) China attacks or blockades Taiwan.

These publicly deliberated issues confirm the view of America’s adversaries: U.S. resources are being worn down and eventually weakened beyond timely recovery for the next foray, be it China against Taiwan, the Philippines or Japan, North Korea against the South, or in Iran opposing U.S. initiatives.

U.S. weapons and munitions replenishment capacity is inherently limited by our system of democratic governance — rule of law, separation of powers, consent of the governed. That system, built on mutual trust and respect between and among its institutions, has been sorely tested in recent years, and subjected to further spasms of division and retribution by Trump’s defiant and quasi-authoritarian decision to take the nation to war without proper consultation with Congress, or with allies from whom Trump is now demanding unfettered loyalty and obedience.

A more humble, or at least respectful, appeal for congressional funding authority to rebuild U.S. weapons systems and defense capacity will send a healthy message, both domestically and to foreign audiences, that the tone-deaf, unilateralist, even arrogant governing style is over. With his bridges to domestic political foes and skeptical, and insulted, allies at least partially repaired, Trump will be in a sounder position to ask for appropriate bipartisan funding and authorization support, as well as allied participation in the existential moment that is now upon America and the West.

As for restoring the right level of credible deterrence with U,S. adversaries, those corrective actions will accomplish more than further empty boasting and bluffing about America’s supremacy and invincibility, by quietly restoring an infusion of arms and a more bipartisan base of support. Domestic American unity will send a chastening message to U.S. adversaries that time and patience are not on their side.

It could also turn a near-national catastrophe to a healing outcome, an opportunity Trump squandered after the first presidential assassination attempt. If, instead, he fails again to seize the moment and bring the country together, it will raise serious doubts as to whose side he is on in the existential struggle between the authoritarians and the West. 

Joseph Bosco served as China country director for the secretary of Defense from 2005 to 2006 and as Asia-Pacific director of humanitarian assistance and disaster relief from 2009 to 2010. He is a nonresident fellow at the Institute for Corean-American Studies, a member of the advisory board of the Global Taiwan Institute and member of the advisory board of the Vandenberg Coalition.

data.title: Man who kept eating salad during WHCA dinner shooting says he 'wasn't scared'
data.description:
One man who did not panic after shots were fired in the vicinity of the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner on Saturday was Michael Glantz.

Glantz, an agent at the Creative Artists Agency, was captured on CNN calmly sitting at his table and eating salad in the aftermath of the shooting. 

Just after 8:30 p.m. EDT, an armed man charged a security checkpoint and exchanged shots with law enforcement officers. One Secret Service officer was struck but saved by a protective vest.

As for why Glantz appeared undisturbed by the commotion, he gave one reason: “I’m a New Yorker.”

“We live with sirens and activity happening all the time,” he told The New York Times on Sunday. “I wasn’t scared. There are hundreds of Secret Service agents hurtling themselves over tables and chairs, and I wanted to watch.”

When shots were first fired on the floor above the ballroom, many guests ducked for cover under their tables. President Trump, Vice President Vance and other administration officials in attendance, meanwhile, were whisked away by law enforcement personnel.

Glantz said that others asked him why he didn’t get on the floor. His explanation for that was also simple.

“First of all, I have a bad back,” he told the Times. “I couldn’t get on the floor, and if I did get on the floor, they’d have to bring in people to get me off the floor. And No. 2, I’m a hygiene freak. There was no freaking way I was getting in my new tux on the dirty Hilton floor. It was not happening.”

The suspect in the shooting, 31-year-old Cole Allen, was charged Monday with attempting to assassinate Trump, transporting a firearm or ammunition in interstate commerce with the intent to commit a felony and discharge of a firearm during a crime of violence. He did not enter a plea.

data.title: Live updates: Trumps welcome British royals; GOP wrestles over DHS funding, FISA
data.description:
The funding battle over the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is front and center on Capitol Hill after Saturday’s shooting at the annual White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

The third assassination attempt on Trump has completely upended the political calculus amid a looming pay freeze for DHS employees. And it has Republicans pushing for the U.S. to fund the president’s $400 million White House ballroom project.

Both chambers, meanwhile, are trying to pass a renewal of Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, which expires Thursday.

Trump and first lady Melania Trump welcomed King Charles III and Queen Camilla at the White House on Tuesday morning. The king will deliver an address to a joint meeting of Congress on Tuesday afternoon, ahead of a state dinner at the White House, all against a backdrop of U.S.-U.K. tension.

In Florida, a GOP-led redistricting session kicks off Tuesday. Here’s what to watch as the national redistricting saga draws to a close.

Trump and first lady Melania Trump will greet Charles and Camilla in the morning at the White House for a tour and meeting, followed by a state dinner Tuesday night.

Follow live below.

data.title: Russian superyacht sails through blockaded Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
A Russian-flagged superyacht sailed through the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend, one of only a few private vessels to pass through the blockaded waterway since the start of the U.S. conflict with Iran, according to maritime traffic data.

Data from the MarineTraffic platform showed that the nearly 142-meter-long Nord left a Dubai port on Friday, sailed through the strait and arrived at a port in Muscat, Oman, on Sunday morning local time. The yacht is linked to sanctioned Russian billionaire Alexey Mordashov.

Tehran has restricted traffic through the strait for two months as part of its retaliatory campaign against the U.S. and Israel, injecting uncertainty into the global oil market. The channel transports about one-fifth of the world’s oil supply daily during peacetime.

Iran has fired upon several vessels attempting to pass through in recent days, while the U.S. has ordered dozens of ships back to port and intercepted at least one Iranian vessel as part of its naval blockade.

President Trump has also ordered the U.S. military to “shoot and kill” any boat caught laying mines in the waterway as so-called U.S. “mine sweepers” work to clear Iranian mines from the channel.   

Russia has benefited from the standoff, raking in hundreds of millions of dollars more than usual from oil sales since the outset of the conflict, due to the Trump administration easing sanctions in an attempt to stave off a worldwide energy crisis.

President Vladimir Putin hosted Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, one of Tehran’s lead negotiators in ceasefire talks, for a meeting in Moscow on Monday during which the two officials reaffirmed deep ties.

Russian state-owned media TASS reported that Putin said Moscow “intends to maintain” strategic relations with Iran and would do everything in its power to achieve peace in the Middle East.

The meeting came after Iran refused to commit to another round of direct talks with U.S. officials to end hostilities, instead conveying their views to Pakistani intermediaries in Islamabad over the weekend before departing the country.

A U.S. delegation, led by special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, was also set to arrive in Pakistan on Saturday, but Trump called off the trip.

“Too much time wasted on traveling, too much work! Besides which, there is tremendous infighting and confusion within their ‘leadership,’”  he wrote in a Truth Social post.  “Nobody knows who is in charge, including them. Also, we have all the cards, they have none! If they want to talk, all they have to do is call!!!”

The Iranians have reportedly offered to loosen their grip on the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for the U.S. ending its naval blockade, but want to defer discussions on its nuclear program — a proposal deemed “unacceptable” by Secretary of State Marco Rubio.

“We can’t let them get away with it,” Rubio told Fox News host Trey Yingst in an interview on Monday. “They’re very good negotiators. They’re very experienced negotiators, and we have to ensure that any deal that is made, any agreement that is made, is one that definitely prevents them from sprinting towards a nuclear weapon at any point.”

data.title: Trump says Iran wants Strait of Hormuz open
data.description:
President Trump said Tuesday that Iran told the U.S. that it is collapsing and wants the Strait of Hormuz opened.

“Iran has just informed us that they are in a ‘State of Collapse,’” Trump wrote on Truth Social. “They want us to ‘Open the Hormuz Strait,’ as soon as possible, as they try to figure out their leadership situation (Which I believe they will be able to do!).”

The president did not say who told him or if he will comply.

The post comes a day after a proposal from Iran was viewed by U.S. officials, including Trump. The reported proposal would end the war and reopen the strait but postpone any nuclear discussions.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio pooh-poohed the proposal Monday, saying Iran’s nuclear program “is the reason why we’re in this in the first place.”

“There’s no doubt in my mind that at some point in the future, if this radical clerical regime remain in charge in Iran, they will decide they want a nuclear weapon,” Rubio told Fox News. “That fundamental issue still has to be confronted. That still remains the core issue here.”

Trump over the weekend stopped the U.S. delegation from traveling to Pakistan for new negotiations. Special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were expected to fly to Islamabad without Vice President Vance.

The president told reporters on Saturday that Iran “gave us a paper that should have been better,” and that 10 minutes after he canceled Witkoff and Kushner’s trip, “we got a new paper that was much better,” according to Bloomberg.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt also confirmed that the president had seen the proposal while at the press briefing Monday.

“I don’t want to get ahead of the president or his national security team,” she said. “What I will reiterate is that the president’s red lines with respect to Iran have been made very, very clear not just to the American public, but also to them as well.”
